sources: Halson SL: Sleep in Elite Athletes and Nutritional Interventions (Sports Med, 2014)
ISSN Position Stand: Protein and Exercise (JISSN, 2017) — doi:10.1186/s12970-017-0177-8
Res PT et al: Protein ingestion before sleep improves post-exercise overnight recovery (Med Sci Sports Exerc, 2012)
note: Sleep is the single most impactful recovery intervention available. Nutrition can support but not replace 7–9 hours of quality sleep.
why_sleep_matters_for_fitness: Growth hormone secretion peaks during deep sleep — critical for muscle repair
Cortisol regulation: poor sleep elevates cortisol, driving muscle breakdown and fat storage
Glycogen replenishment is impaired by sleep deprivation
Motor learning and skill consolidation happen during sleep — relevant for sport and exercise technique

protein_before_bed.dose: 30–40 g casein or slow-digesting plant protein 30–60 min before sleep
protein_before_bed.why: Stimulates muscle protein synthesis overnight. Res et al (2012) showed improved next-day recovery.
vegetarian_sources: Cottage cheese (casein-rich, but contains lactose — use lactose-free version)
Tofu with hemp seeds (~25–30 g protein, slower digesting than whey)

tryptophan_foods.why: Tryptophan is the amino acid precursor to serotonin → melatonin. Eating tryptophan-rich foods with a small carb serving increases brain uptake.
tryptophan_foods.carb_pairing: A small serve of complex carbs (oats, banana) with the tryptophan food improves uptake across the blood-brain barrier.

magnesium.why: Supports GABA (calming neurotransmitter), reduces cortisol, improves sleep quality.

pre_sleep_nutrition.timing: Avoid large meals within 2–3 hours of sleep — elevated insulin and body temperature impair sleep onset.
avoid_before_bed: Caffeine within 6 hours of sleep (half-life ~5 hours)
Alcohol — impairs sleep quality and REM despite aiding sleep onset
High-fat heavy meals — delayed gastric emptying causes discomfort and raises core temperature
Very high-fibre foods — fermentation and gas production can disturb sleep

next_morning_recovery_nutrition.rehydrate_first: Overnight fluid loss is ~500ml–1L. Drink 500ml water before breakfast.
next_morning_recovery_nutrition.protein_at_breakfast: 25–30 g protein at breakfast extends overnight MPS benefit from the pre-sleep dose.
next_morning_recovery_nutrition.carbs: Replenish glycogen if training the same day. Oats, fruit, whole grain toast.
next_morning_recovery_nutrition.avoid: Skipping breakfast after hard training days — delays recovery and increases muscle breakdown

signs_poor_sleep_affecting_recovery: Persistent soreness beyond 48 hours after training
Appetite increases, especially for sugar and refined carbs

quick_pre_sleep_options_for_vegetarians.option: Lactose-free Greek yogurt with a small banana
quick_pre_sleep_options_for_vegetarians.why: Casein protein + tryptophan from banana + small carb for uptake

quick_pre_sleep_options_for_vegetarians.option: Tofu with a handful of pumpkin seeds
quick_pre_sleep_options_for_vegetarians.why: Plant casein-equivalent + magnesium from pumpkin seeds

quick_pre_sleep_options_for_vegetarians.option: Small bowl of oats with hemp seeds
quick_pre_sleep_options_for_vegetarians.why: Tryptophan + slow carbs + magnesium; gentle on the stomach
